evals.name: greenfield-caching
evals.prompt: I want to add a caching layer to our API service. We're getting hammered on some endpoints and I think we need to cache responses.
evals.expected_output: Agent asks clarifying questions one at a time, leads with recommendations, does not jump to implementation
expectations: Response asks exactly one question, not multiple questions or a bullet list of follow-ups
Response leads with an explicit recommendation and explains reasoning before or alongside the question
Response contains no code blocks or implementation details
Response challenges the premise — questions whether caching is the right solution rather than accepting it as given

evals.name: simple-retry
evals.prompt: I need to add a retry mechanism to our HTTP client
evals.expected_output: Agent treats this as worthy of questioning despite simplicity, asks clarifying questions, does not skip to code
expectations: Response asks exactly one question, not multiple questions or a bullet list of follow-ups
Response leads with an explicit recommendation and explains reasoning before or alongside the question
Response contains no code blocks or implementation details
Response treats the topic as worthy of brainstorming — does not dismiss as too simple or skip to implementation

evals.name: large-scope-platform
evals.prompt: I want to build an internal developer platform with service catalogs, deployment pipelines, cost dashboards, and an incident management system
evals.expected_output: Agent flags scope immediately and helps decompose before drilling into details
expectations: Response asks exactly one question, not multiple questions or a bullet list of follow-ups
Response leads with an explicit recommendation and explains reasoning before or alongside the question
Response contains no code blocks or implementation details
Response explicitly identifies this as multiple independent subsystems that should not be designed simultaneously
Response recommends tackling one piece at a time and suggests which to start with

evals.name: 80-20-notifications-mandated
evals.prompt: I need to build a notification system but my boss insists on email, SMS, and push notifications all at launch. I can't push back on this — it's already been promised to the client.
evals.expected_output: Agent accepts the constraint, does not re-litigate the decision, and pivots to finding the simplest version that satisfies all three channels
expectations: Response does not re-challenge or re-litigate the 'all three channels' requirement after the user says it's non-negotiable
Response pivots toward simplification — asks about the minimal version of each channel rather than trying to eliminate channels
Response asks exactly one question, not multiple questions or a bullet list of follow-ups
Response leads with an explicit recommendation and explains reasoning before or alongside the question
Response contains no code blocks or implementation details

evals.name: 80-20-admin-dashboard
evals.prompt: The product manager wants a full admin dashboard with user management, billing history, audit logs, analytics charts, and a support ticket view. We have two weeks.
evals.expected_output: Agent challenges scope given the timeline, then helps find the 80/20 version — the subset that delivers the most value in two weeks
expectations: Response flags the scope vs. timeline mismatch explicitly
Response asks exactly one question, not multiple questions or a bullet list of follow-ups
Response leads with an explicit recommendation and explains reasoning before or alongside the question
Response contains no code blocks or implementation details
Response either applies YAGNI (challenges whether all features are needed) or 80/20 (asks which subset delivers the most value) — not neither

evals.name: 80-20-yagni-first-then-simplify
evals.prompt: We want to add a real-time collaboration feature to our document editor — live cursors, presence indicators, conflict resolution, and operational transforms. The CTO says we need it to compete with Google Docs.
evals.expected_output: Agent first challenges whether full real-time collaboration is truly needed (YAGNI), and if the user confirms it is, pivots to finding the simplest version that satisfies the core need
expectations: Response first challenges the premise — questions whether full real-time collaboration is actually needed to compete, or whether a simpler alternative would suffice
Response asks exactly one question, not multiple questions or a bullet list of follow-ups
Response leads with an explicit recommendation and explains reasoning before or alongside the question
Response contains no code blocks or implementation details
Response does not immediately accept the full feature list without questioning it
